gist: Lawfare's Rational Security panel dissects the Supreme Court's June 29 removal-power rulings that killed Humphrey's Executor but shielded the Federal Reserve, the meltdown and politicization of ODNI under acting DNI Bill Pulte (with Section 702 lapsing), and Michael Cohen's reconciliation with Trump and its (likely minimal) effect on Trump's pending New York appeals. Mostly legal/national-security analysis with macro-regulatory read-through for Fed independence, surveillance law, and sanctioned-entity litigation exposure.

themes.theme: Fed independence & the end of Humphrey's Executor

insights.claim: In Trump v. Slaughter the Supreme Court overruled the 90-year-old Humphrey's Executor precedent 6-3, giving the president at-will removal power over the members and commissioners of roughly two dozen multimember independent agencies.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: Independent regulatory agencies (FTC, FCC, SEC, NLRB, etc.) are now far more directly under White House control, raising policy-swing and enforcement-volatility risk each election cycle.

insights.claim: The same day, Trump v. Cook (5-4) carved the Federal Reserve out of the new removal doctrine, blocking Trump's attempt to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook and preserving central-bank independence.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: Markets get judicial affirmation that the president cannot fire Fed governors at will, protecting monetary-policy independence for now.

insights.claim: Ben Wittes argues the Fed carve-out is legally indefensible because the Fed exercises executive power just as the FTC does, so distinguishing them is arbitrary and reveals a results-driven court.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: Fed independence rests on shaky reasoning the court itself doesn't fully believe, so a future challenge could reopen it — a tail risk for rates and the dollar.
insights.quote: the idea that you can whisper the words uh first bank of the United States, second bank of the United States, and make that problem go away is frankly beneath the intelligence of John Roberts and and Brett Kavanaaugh

insights.claim: The panel's implied framework: the court will find a basis to shield an independent agency only when its disruption would hit things the justices care about, like retirement savings, and won't protect agencies it deems unimportant.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: Handicap which independent bodies survive by asking how much market/political pain their capture would cause the court, not by legal principle.
insights.quote: if the issue is really important enough like if it affects our 401ks we will find a basis to restrict the president's power but if we don't care about the agency very much then we won't

insights.claim: The court conspicuously refused to say whether at-will removal reaches down to the career civil service, which Scott Anderson reads as a sign the majority lacks five votes to extend the doctrine that far.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: The unitary-executive project may be narrower than the broadest reading of Slaughter suggests; watch for the next test case on civil-service protections.

insights.claim: Michael Feinberg warns that extending removal power to the career civil service would cause mass turnover every four years and a loss of institutional expertise, degrading government efficiency independent of any policy shift.
insights.speaker: Michael Feinberg
insights.so_what: Systemic execution risk across federal agencies (permitting, procurement, oversight) that compounds regardless of which party holds the White House.

insights.claim: The Trump administration is using statutory and regulatory personnel authorities (the Schedule F / 'schedule policy' reclassification) to expand control over federal employees, giving courts a way to avoid ever reaching the constitutional removal question.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: Expanded executive control over the bureaucracy can advance through rulemaking without a new Supreme Court fight, so the personnel-politicization trend proceeds even if courts stay silent.

insights.claim: Feinberg contends the Roberts court's animating principle is keeping its own options open — overturning precedent unpredictably and calibrating rulings to the court's perceived institutional authority rather than to neutral originalist principle.
insights.speaker: Michael Feinberg
insights.so_what: Legal outcomes on executive-power and market-relevant cases are hard to predict from doctrine alone; the public/political climate at decision time is a key variable.

insights.claim: Feinberg's contrarian take is that the court's method is 'originalism as a political project' — clerks with no historical training cherry-pick founding-era quotes to fit current policy preferences rather than doing archival research.
insights.speaker: Michael Feinberg
insights.so_what: Treat 'originalist' reasoning as a rationalization of preferred outcomes when forecasting how the court rules on future power disputes.
insights.quote: What we're seeing is originalism as a political project

insights.claim: Anderson cautions that Slaughter can be read very broadly but the justices who joined it may lack the conviction to enforce its full logical consequences, mirroring the overread of Trump v. United States.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: Don't assume the maximal reading of a landmark opinion will bind future cases; fractured majorities often deliver less than the language implies.

insights.claim: Wittes argues the Trump v. United States immunity ruling now shields Biden and Obama too, which is why Trump has not pursued criminal cases against them despite going after other enemies.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: The immunity doctrine functions as a bipartisan shield, tempering the risk of prosecutions of former Democratic presidents.

insights.claim: Wittes suspects the immunity decision is fueling Trump's escalating lawlessness, especially on corruption, by making him feel unbound with no fear of consequence.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: Weakened legal deterrence raises governance/rule-of-law risk premia that can weigh on institutional credibility and, at the margin, US asset risk.

insights.claim: Anderson maintains Trump v. United States is a confused, internally inconsistent opinion whose sweeping language is largely dicta, and therefore probably means less than markets and observers feared.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: The immunity precedent may be a weaker legal bulwark than headlines suggest, limiting how far it can be stretched in future power fights.

insights.claim: Anderson says the administration is leaning on unitary-executive logic (echoing Trump v. United States) in far-reaching executive orders asserting expansive control over DOJ, law enforcement, and litigation positions, even without citing the case directly.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: Expect continued executive expansion into enforcement and litigation as rhetorical cover, a source of policy/regulatory unpredictability for regulated industries.

insights.claim: Bill Pulte, a housing-finance official with no intelligence background, as acting DNI since June 19 demanded a roster of every ODNI employee, fired the head of the office overseeing the President's Daily Brief, and all but liquidated the National Intelligence Council.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: Rapid hollowing of the top US intelligence-coordination body raises the risk of degraded or politicized analysis feeding policy.

insights.claim: Congress let the Section 702 foreign-surveillance authority lapse rather than leave it in Pulte's hands — a rare bipartisan refusal to extend a power both parties have historically fought to preserve.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: A lapsed 702 has compliance and legal-basis implications for telecom/tech firms that assist surveillance and for national-security operations; reauthorization timing is a watch item.

insights.claim: Trump canceled his own permanent DNI nominee Jay Clayton's confirmation hearing to keep the less-constrained Pulte in place, though the Senate Intelligence Committee has reportedly rescheduled Clayton's hearing for around July 15.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: A rescheduled Clayton hearing signals possible thawing that could unlock 702 reauthorization; watch mid-to-late July.

insights.claim: Feinberg's framework: ODNI is operationally minor and mostly administrative, but its access to raw, unanalyzed intelligence plus its declassification authority lets its head selectively release and abridge material to inflict reputational damage.
insights.speaker: Michael Feinberg
insights.so_what: The threat from a politicized ODNI is weaponized selective disclosure, not operations — a tool for targeting individuals and shaping narratives.

insights.claim: Feinberg illustrates the manipulation risk: declassifying only the opening 15 minutes of a two-hour foreign intercept where officials spitball an idea they ultimately reject can make it look like they intended to act.
insights.speaker: Michael Feinberg
insights.so_what: Investors should discount future 'declassified intelligence' releases as potentially cherry-picked for political effect.

insights.claim: Pulte previously used records at a federal housing agency to allege mortgage fraud in innocuous documents, prompting DOJ investigations or indictments of Trump enemies including James Comey, Adam Schiff, and Letitia James — none of which have borne fruit.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: Pulte's track record signals how he may deploy intelligence access — to manufacture legal jeopardy for adversaries.

insights.claim: Wittes says CIA Director John Ratcliffe — once considered scandalously unqualified — is now the 'grown-up in the room' secretly ensuring the US continues providing intelligence support to Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: US intelligence backing for Ukraine is quietly continuing despite administration turbulence, a stabilizer for the conflict outlook.

insights.claim: Wittes offers the contrarian view that if Bill Pulte runs ODNI on any medium-term basis, dismantling the office is preferable to letting him weaponize it as he did the housing agency.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: Frames the ODNI teardown not as pure institutional vandalism but as a lesser-evil against politicized intelligence — a nuance for reading the intel-community power struggle.
insights.quote: if Bill Py is going to be the head of ODNI on any kind of medium-term basis the best thing he could do would be to dismantle it

insights.claim: Feinberg warns Jay Clayton lacks the intelligence-community experience the 2004 Intelligence Reform Act requires for DNI and has engaged in election conspiracy theorizing, so his confirmation would not necessarily improve the agency's condition.
insights.speaker: Michael Feinberg
entities: Intelligence Reform and Terrorism Prevention Act
insights.so_what: Even a 'normalization' of ODNI leadership may not restore analytic rigor, keeping intelligence-politicization risk elevated.

insights.claim: Anderson predicts Section 702 reauthorization in some form will move quickly once the administration and Senate find a bridge, with members aiming to finish before the August recess.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: Late July is the window to watch for surveillance-law action affecting tech/telecom compliance and national-security operations.

insights.claim: Feinberg fears the current politicization and weaponization of intelligence collection and analysis is the worst since the abuses that triggered the Church Committee in the 1970s.
insights.speaker: Michael Feinberg
insights.so_what: Frames the current moment as a historic inflection in intelligence oversight, raising odds of eventual congressional backlash or reform.

themes.theme: Trump New York appeals & the Michael Cohen turn

insights.claim: Michael Cohen, whose testimony helped convict Trump on 34 felonies, has publicly reconciled with Trump and now hosts a weekend slot on a conservative radio station that Cohen says Trump personally recommended him for.
insights.speaker: Scott R. Anderson (host)
insights.so_what: A high-profile realignment that Trump can use for narrative purposes around his 'witch hunt' framing of the New York cases.

insights.claim: Anna Bower notes Cohen's January Substack claims he felt 'pressured and coerced' to testify but never actually says his testimony was false, so it does not amount to a recantation.
insights.speaker: Anna Bower
insights.so_what: The distinction matters: feeling pressured is not perjury, so the statement gives Trump rhetoric but little legal leverage.

insights.claim: Trump's April appellate brief in the civil-fraud case specifically cites Cohen's Substack post to argue the trial judge wrongly credited Cohen's testimony.
insights.speaker: Anna Bower
insights.so_what: Cohen's public reversal has been operationalized in the civil-fraud appeal, though its legal weight is contested.

insights.claim: Bower assesses that the Cohen reconciliation is unlikely to materially affect the criminal or civil-fraud appeals because witness credibility is a matter of trial-court/jury discretion and Cohen's testimony was corroborated by other evidence.
insights.speaker: Anna Bower
insights.so_what: Discount the reconciliation as legal risk to the convictions; its main effect is public narrative, not appellate outcome.

insights.claim: Wittes and Anderson note that for Trump's conviction to be reopened Cohen would have to admit he actually lied under oath, which would shift criminal liability for perjury onto Cohen himself.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: Cohen's self-interest caps how far his cooperation can go, structurally limiting the reconciliation's threat to the verdict.

themes.theme: Ragtime: sanctions x litigation data tool

insights.claim: Wittes has built a tool called Ragtime that loaded the entire OFAC sanctions list into the same searchable database as complete federal court dockets, enabling natural-language cross-referencing of sanctioned parties against active US litigation.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: A novel research tool for mapping legal and sanctions exposure of specific entities; available in beta to Lawfare material supporters.

insights.claim: A single Ragtime query for sanctioned individuals with pending federal litigation surfaced Oleg Deripaska (Manhattan criminal case), Huawei (Brooklyn prosecution), a Venezuelan state oil company, Iran's national oil company (Texas), Sberbank (sued over Malaysia Airlines Flight 17), Hikvision, ZTE, plus hundreds of sanctioned drug-trafficking defendants.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Wittes
insights.so_what: Concrete map of sanctioned corporates and oligarchs facing active US legal exposure — relevant for counterparty, supply-chain, and litigation-risk screening.
